transcript: I'm Brennan with Anza. And uh at Anza we are very influential in helping steer core protocol development for Solana. Uh most notably we build the Agave client, which is the software that most of the validators on the network run. And so uh we can think of this as the base platform upon which all this other really cool stuff is built. And during the next looks like nine minutes here, uh we're going to go on a journey to recap a few exciting wins, but uh more importantly we're going to illuminate the path of where we're taking the core protocol. And explain why Solana continues to command the premier trading experience powered by Agave under the hood. And we're going to structure things by looking at these emergent themes that have dominated core developer focus uh at different times. And um so we'll march forward in time and check in on how we're doing from a resiliency and performance perspective, before we dive into the exciting new market structure changes that are on the horizon. Let's go ahead and start with resiliency. That's a lot of green. Almost two years of solid green. And around 200 billion transactions. And not that anyone's keeping score, but, if you were, that's more transactions than pretty much every other chain. Combined. In their entire history. Right, Solana is Lindy. Solana stays up and keeps servicing transactions. And that's been the case for a long time now. Um a couple months back on October 10th, uh and earlier this year with the Trump coin launch, serve as two proof points of you know really the maturity of not only the Agave client, but the Solana ecosystem at large. Um and at Anza, you know, one of the things that gets us really excited is keeping those big economic event days super boring. And October 10th it was a lot of things, but from an engineering perspective, it was super boring. And I think that's a giant W for this community. And not only has uptime been fantastic, but we have kept innovating and shipping uh at this at you know torrid pace. With quarterly releases coming out like clockwork. But, we must refuse to get complacent. Right we've seen giants like AWS and Cloudflare have issues this year that have brought you know businesses to their knees. And meanwhile while this is going on Solana has just kept humming along. And to get ahead of issues, Anza conducts immense uh stress testing, red teaming, and of course situation monitoring. The Invalidator team at Anza is attacking the Solana testnet with a unique set of tests every hour. And these attacks mostly fall into two major camps. The first one deals with uh network DoS. And so we're really trying to stress that backpressure handling and load shedding and things of that nature. And the second big bucket deals with crafting these curated blocks that stress the far corners of the Solana Virtual Machine and replay execution. We spend a lot of time thinking about what could the most malicious, uh, you know, informed, reasonably staked validator do to the network, and then what could you know some pretty well capitalized, very smart, user dev do to break the network. And so we build out all of these types of scenarios and then build the defenses against them. And all of the major Solana network protocols, the internal ones, are covered and fortified under this testing. Here's just one example of us hosing down testnet nodes with gigabits of traffic. But meanwhile the network is just screaming along continuing to make blocks on time. And attacks like this used to knock us offline, now, they barely register. And it's only this level of paranoia and obsession that allows us to keep shipping quickly and uh survive while doing it. And we've been shipping a lot. 2025 has been the year of increased bandwidth reduced latency or IBRL for Solana. And there's a lot to cover here so let's get into it. We've increased the transaction throughput. Block capacity is how we constrain the transactions going into each block, so we can keep those blocks flowing out to the network every 400 milliseconds, or sometimes faster. And we've increased overall throughput by 25% this year, which means more user transactions per slot without delaying confirmation time. And we also doubled the amount of throughput to the hottest accounts. These are the most popular assets or asset pairs that get hammered during those economic events. And this chart shows when that limit was doubled from 12 million to 24 million compute units, and how users immediately gobbled up that extra burst capacity. Programs and accounts that used to bottleneck the whole chain, don't anymore. But we're not done. The next throughput increase will double overall throughput and take us to 100 million compute units per block. And the key feature enabling this is bypassing the Linux kernel at the network layer. Agave is too fast to be waiting around to copy data or you know waiting for threads to switch out. Um and uh you know XDP increases the throughput of the primary bottleneck on the network by 200x. So this chart shows the latency difference between the legacy Agave code, that blue line at the top, and the new and improved uh Agave code which is that purple line way down there at the bottom. And notice we had to make the axis the uh y-axis log scale so that you would even be able to see this purple line at all. This thing is super fast. Okay, call your validator today, tell them to run XDP, let's make it rain blockspace. In addition to increasing the actual uh block capacity, we've increased the ingestion TPS. Sustaining over 100k TPS during some periods on mainnet. And ingestion TPS is essentially a measure of how fast we can get bidders into the auction house. Making sure the highest bidders actually win the bid is essential for building an efficient market. And you can't identify the best bids if you don't look at all the bids, okay, obviously. And so Agave has taken a giant leap here this year. And finally on the performance and resilience fronts, Alpenglow is coming. Alpenglow is the brand new consensus engine we're dropping in to replace Tower BFT. And it will take transaction finality times down to 150 milliseconds or less. That's a level of snappiness that your users can really feel. And it also comes with increased safety in regards to tightly controlling some of these timings and handling things like equivocation. Uh we first announced Alpenglow in the middle of this year at Accelerate, uh but we've been running a 50 node globally distributed test cluster under load for the last greater than four months. So we've made huge strides here. We've had so few problems in fact that I've had to like log into these machines and make sure that something's actually running cause we're just having not that many problems. I mean this is fantastic stuff. And the team's working insanely hard to get it feature complete by the end of the year and rolled out on testnet early next year. And Alpenglow sets the foundation... yeah you can clap that's okay. All right. Alpenglow really sets the foundation for a bunch of more exciting things to come. And this is where things get really exciting. Building the absolute best economics and market structure is the newest emergent theme. Solana showed everyone that blockchains can be fast and affordable, and now Anza and the core devs are going to show that they can be fair and highly efficient while remaining trustless. So how are we going to do it? It starts with Multiple Concurrent Proposers, or MCP. We are ending the single builder monopoly. Users and dApps want optionality and regional network latencies, traders want to have confidence that they're playing a fair game with censorship resistance and transaction hiding properties, and MCP will deliver all of these things. And we're already incrementally building towards this vision. We'll start shipping features to move closer to this end ideal state with each release in the coming year. And what I love about Solana is the developer community's ability to take these big audacious ideas and translate it into shippable code in record time. Switching gears. We've heard the cries from the community. The rent is too damn high. And we are lowering the cost of state on the blockchain. Yeah. I know. A lot of people very passionate about this. It'll be more affordable for projects to bootstrap huge numbers of users and accounts. And the devs have designed a simple scheme to allow us uh to adjust the cost on-chain in response to changing environmental factors. And this isn't some modest decrease that we're talking about. We're going to drop a zero from the price. Maybe two. We're committed to making Solana the absolute best place for builders to build. And finally, we're making it simpler for validators to share revenue from different sources. With stakers and infra providers in-protocol. Delegated stake directly increases the number of blocks that a validator can build, and we're going to drive a ton of trading activity to Solana, so it's important that those stakers have an in-protocol way of participating in those rewards as well. So let's revisit these overarching themes. Solana is resilient, with a track record of staying up and handling immense load while continuing to ship quickly. Solana is high performance, and only getting faster, delivering high bandwidth and low latency where it counts. And now Solana is embarking on an audacious journey to build the best market structure in the game, where traders can compete on a level playing field and builders can be confident the chain and the economics will scale with their application. A single, global, unified state machine, synchronizing at the speed of light, and facilitating the premier trading experience for any asset. This is the future state of the network.

* [Threat Research173](https://research.checkpoint.com/category/threat-research-2/)

![](https://research.checkpoint.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/balancer-v2-thumb-1024x1024px.jpg)
# How an Attacker Drained $128M from Balancer Through Rounding Error Exploitation
November 5, 2025
[]()[]()[]()
https://research.checkpoint.com/2025/how-an-attacker-drained-128m-from-balancer-through-rounding-error-exploitation/
*By: Dikla Barda, Roaman Zaikin & Oded Vanunu*
On November 3, 2025, Check Point Research’s blockchain monitoring systems detected a sophisticated exploit targeting Balancer V2’s ComposableStablePool contracts. The attacker exploited arithmetic precision loss in pool invariant calculations to drain**$128.64 million**across six blockchain networks in under 30 minutes.
The attack leveraged a rounding error vulnerability in the \_upscaleArray function that, when combined with carefully crafted batchSwap operations, allowed the attacker to artificially suppress BPT (Balancer Pool Token) prices and extract value through repeated arbitrage cycles. The exploitation occurred primarily during attacker smart contract deployment, with the constructor executing 65+ micro-swaps that compounded precision loss to devastating effect.
## **Introduction**
In the early morning hours of November 3, 2025, Check Point’s Blockchain Threat Analysis system flagged unusual activity on the Ethereum mainnet involving Balancer’s V2 Vault contract. Within minutes, our automated detection identified a critical exploit in progress, with massive fund outflows from multiple liquidity pools.
![Balancer V2](https://research.checkpoint.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/balancer-v2-1-1090px-1024x366.png)
The attack exploited a mathematical vulnerability in how Balancer’s ComposableStablePools handle small-value swaps. When token balances are pushed to specific rounding boundaries (8-9 wei range), Solidity’s integer division causes significant precision loss. The attacker weaponized this by executing batched swap sequences that accumulated these tiny errors into catastrophic invariant manipulation.
## **Background: Balancer V2 Architecture**
### **The Vault System**
Balancer V2 uses a centralized “Vault” contract (0xBA12222222228d8Ba445958a75a0704d566BF2C8) that holds all tokens across all pools, separating token storage from pool logic to reduce gas costs and enable capital efficiency.

sources.title: VALORANT Patch Notes 11.10
sources.snippet:
VALORANT Patch Notes 11.10
![](data:image/svg+xml,<svg%20xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"/>)
![](data:image/svg+xml,<svg%20xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"/>)
# VALORANT Patch Notes 11.10
The Harbor Rework is here! Along with QoL updates, balance updates to Clove, and bug fixes.
[Game Updates](https://playvalorant.com/en-gb/news/game-updates/)
Kenny “Karnifexlol“ Cameron
2025-11-11T14:00:00.000Z
Sup, gamers? Kenny, here.
Soooo…who’s ready for that Raja Rework?
That’s right THE HARBOR REWORK IS HERE!
Read below for full details on the Harbor rework as well as a bunch of other stuff including:
* The Team HUD is getting some Healing visibility Improvements
* New UI layouts are in the Collections pages
* Balance updates to Clove
* Pick’Ems is coming to GameChangers on November 13!
* The Replay System is ready for Consoles!
* And a bunch of bug fixes.
Check these notes, then hop in-game and make some waves! GL HF GGs.
# **ALL PLATFORMS**
## GENERAL UPDATES
* Team HUD Health Improvements
* Healing Indicator: The top HUD health bar now shows when you or an ally are healing. During recovery, the bar turns teal and fills to reflect healing progress. Once recovered, the bar returns to its normal white color.
* We've enhanced the Collections experience for both PC & Console, bringing you improvements such as:
* A grid layout is in, and carousel is out for PC Weapons & Buddies
* New dedicated page for equipping and viewing titles on PC.
* Custom skinline backgrounds across more Collections screens; letting your favorite weapon skins shine
* *Note: the above changes will be delayed for our players in Korea until 11.11 following some player flow changes*## AGENT UPDATES
* **Harbor**
* The Harbor Rework is finally here! We’ve overhauled Harbor’s abilities to move him into a more aggressive Controller, with a*splash*of Initiator. High Tide was an iconic part of his kit and we wanted players to be able to carry over their mastery and creativity from High Tide casts in the past. We’ve moved Cove into the signature slot with a few changes to make it easier to place with precision. This should help Harbor find more ways to block vision for his allies from afar or Shield them depending on the situation. Harbor will be gaining a new ability to help with aggressively taking gun fights, Storm Surge! Reckoning also received a massive update, making it easier to set up his allies to flood into enemy territory.
* Read the updated Ability Tooltips below, and check out our social channels for more content breaking down Harbor’s reworked abilities. We’re very excited for you to all*dive*in!
* Cove
* Changed to the signature ability slot
* EQUIP Cove. ACTIVATE to form a water Smoke in the select location. HOLD FIRE while targeting to move the marker further away and HOLD ALT FIRE to move it closer. RELOAD to toggle targeting view. REACTIVATE to Shield the water Smoke, blocking any bullets that hit it. The Shielded water Smoke can be destroyed.
* High Tide
* Changed to a non-signature ability
* Cost 300 credits
* Storm Surge (New)
* EQUIP Storm Surge. FIRE to throw, creating an explosive whirlpool that Nearsights and Slows enemies within it after a short duration.
* Reckoning
* EQUIP Reckoning. FIRE to unleash the full power of your artifact, releasing a surge of water that barrels forward to Nearsight and Slow enemies that are hit.
* We've also updated Harbor’s voice lines to reflect his updated abilities. We added conversation voice lines with Tejo and Waylay and have new lines at the start of the match on Abyss and Corrode.
* **Clove**
* Clove is dominating competitive queue pick rates and preventing other controllers from being picked. These changes are aimed at shaving off Clove’s snowballing power. When Clove’s team gets ahead in advantageous states, they very easily outperform other controllers.
* Ruse
* Ruse charges are now restricted to 1 maximum when Clove is dead
* Pick Me Up
* Overheal amount, while Pic

sources.title: Deadman: Annihilation Overview & Rewards Blog
sources.snippet:
## Deadman: Annihilation Overview & Rewards Blog

10 November 2025

_It's that time of year again, Deadman: Annihilation releases January 30th, 2026 and it is back with a bang!_

_A huge thank you to Torvesta, B0aty and Skill Specs for learning all of their lines for this announcement video! If you can't see the video above, [click here to watch it on YouTube](https://youtu.be/d85f0ZVrtQc)._

Gielinor is once again faced with a terrible fate beginning with the letter 'a'. This time around there's no Apocalypse, there's no Armageddon, only certain Annihilation, which is more or less the same thing but with more syllables than ever before! There's nothing you can do to stop it, this world's fate is foretold, all that's left for you to do is gear up and go down swinging so that you might be the last player standing before everything turns to dust. Do you have what it takes?

Today's post focuses on exactly what's changed since our previous event (Deadman: Armageddon) and giving you a brief refresher on the most important bits. That means we'll be covering all of the most important changes at a top-level rather than bombarding you with hundreds of tiny little bullet points (though we'll be putting a blog together before the event to go through all of the finer details for anybody who might be interested!)

Before we jump into all of the exciting new stuff, let's get everybody up to speed with a quick refresher - we've had a lot of new players join the community throughout 2025, after all.

What is Deadman Mode?

For the uninitiated, Deadman Mode is a brutal - yet surprisingly approachable, more on that soon - temporary game mode where PvP is enabled almost everywhere in Gielinor. Our first ever Deadman Mode took place around ten years ago (feel old?) in late 2015 and saw players starting fresh accounts, levelling their stats with boosted XP rates and gearing up for an epic finale, all while remaining constantly on-edge as PvP combat can break out any time, anywhere, at a mere moment's notice.

Players risked their equipped gear, their inventories and their banked loot while progressing and trying to stay ahead of the curve before fighting to be the last player standing in a finale that saw deadly fog sweep across the world and force any survivors into a do-or-die final showdown.

In the years since, Deadman Mode's undergone a number of iterations and countless changes. 2021's Deadman: Reborn saw the introduction of Sigils, powerups similar to Leagues relics, that players could obtain to supercharge their progression or give them an edge in combat, alongside Combat bracket worlds to give players more flexibility in playing specific builds and still being competitive.

2023's Deadman: Apocalypse built on Deadman: Reborn and introduced Breaches, regularly scheduled tears in the fabric of reality where players gathered on mass to slay hordes of randomly spawning bosses for supercharged loot, while trying to stay under the radar and avoid capturing the interest of PKers at the breaches.

2024's Deadman: Armageddon built on the success of Apocalypse with fresh Breach mechanics, shakeups to Sigils and progression, and cosmetic rewards for the main game that players could buy for Deadman Points, earned by doing just about anything within Deadman: Armageddon.

"But I've never done PvP before, this doesn't sound like something for me!"

That brings us to now, a decade since Deadman's inception, with a brand new offering. While 'always on PvP' sounds (and is) scary, we've been taking steps with each iteration to try and make the game mode approachable for players with limited PvP experience while trying to preserve the sense of danger and that 'anything could happen' feeling that makes Deadman or the Wilderness stand out.

So if your PvP experience is limited then don't be discouraged - there's still plenty for you to enjoy in Deadman: Annihilation and this blog should help get you up to speed with everything that's changing. If you're a tot

editorial.headline: Anza Unveils Alpenglow Consensus and Ends Single-Leader Monopoly
editorial.standfirst: Solana is re-architecting its core to deliver sub-150ms finality and a censorship-resistant market structure. The introduction of Multiple Concurrent Proposers marks a pivot from raw speed to institutional-grade fairness.
key_takeaways: **Alpenglow** consensus engine replaces Tower BFT to achieve sub-150ms transaction finality.
**Multiple Concurrent Proposers (MCP)** will eliminate the single-validator leader monopoly, decentralizing block production.
Throughput capacity targets 100M Compute Units per block via **XDP** Linux kernel bypass technology.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
After two years of uninterrupted uptime and processing over 200 billion transactions, **Anza** is shifting its focus from stabilizing the **Solana** network to fundamentally re-architecting its market structure. The headline revelation from Breakpoint 2025 is the introduction of **Multiple Concurrent Proposers (MCP)**, a mechanism designed to dismantle the single-builder monopoly that currently defines block production. By allowing multiple validators to propose blocks simultaneously, **Solana** is moving toward a censorship-resistant model that neutralizes the advantage of geographic latency and ensures a fairer playing field for institutional traders and dApps.

Complementing this structural shift is the deployment of **Alpenglow**, a new consensus engine set to replace the legacy Tower BFT. While **Solana** has long been the fastest chain, **Alpenglow** targets a new frontier: finality times of under 150 milliseconds. This level of "snappiness" is not merely a UX improvement; it closes the arbitrage gap between on-chain trading and centralized exchanges (CEXs). The protocol has been stress-tested on a 50-node globally distributed cluster for over four months, with a testnet rollout scheduled for early 2026. This upgrade positions **Solana** as a single, global state machine synchronizing at the speed of light.

On the performance front, the network is bypassing traditional bottlenecks using **XDP** (eXpress Data Path) technology. By routing data around the Linux kernel, the **Agave** client can now process 100 million compute units per block—a massive leap enabled by eliminating the latency of data copying and thread switching. Simultaneously, **Anza** announced a radical economic restructuring: the cost of state rent is projected to drop by "a zero or two," significantly lowering the barrier for developers to bootstrap high-volume applications like DePIN networks or payments platforms.
